turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Jackson has the opinion of the Court this morning in Case 23-1039, Ames v. the Ohio Department of Youth Services.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Title VII prohibits employers from discriminating against employees on the basis of race, sex, religion, and other protected characteristics. An employee who asserts a claim for unlawful disparate treatment under Title VII must prove that the employer intentionally discriminated against her on one of those protected grounds. Courts typically use a three-step inquiry, commonly known as the McDonnell Douglas framework, to determine whether a Title VII plaintiff has made the requisite showing of discriminatory intent. At the first step of the McDonnell Douglas framework, the plaintiff must make what is called a prima facie case by producing enough evidence to support an inference that the employer acted with a discriminatory motive. If the plaintiff clears that hurdle, then at the second step of the inquiry, the burden shifts to the employer to articulate some legitimate non-discriminatory explanation for the adverse action that it took related to the employee. If the employer provides such a justification, then at the third and final step of the inquiry, the plaintiff has an opportunity to prove that the employer's stated justification is pretextual. This case concerns only the first step of the McDonnell Douglas inquiry, the prima facie case. Ordinarily, the plaintiff's prima facie burden is not onerous. As our cases explain, it's usually enough for a plaintiff to show that she was qualified for a position but was rejected under circumstances that give rise to an inference of discrimination. In this case, however, the Sixth Circuit held that plaintiffs who are members of majority groups bear an additional burden. Under that circuit's precedent, majority group plaintiffs must present evidence of "background circumstances to support a suspicion that the defendant is that unusual employer who discriminates against the majority." The Sixth Circuit has explained that such additional evidence might include evidence that a member of the relevant minority group made the employment decision at issue or statistical evidence showing a pattern of discrimination against members of the majority group. Today, we reject the background circumstances requirement. The petitioner in this case is Marlean Ames, a heterosexual woman who worked for Ohio's Juvenile Corrections Agency. Ames applied to be promoted to a management position within the agency, but was rejected, and a lesbian woman was ultimately selected for the role. Ames was then demoted shortly thereafter, and her old job was given to a gay man. Ames responded by filing this Title VII discrimination lawsuit. She alleged that the agency had discriminated against her on the basis of her sexual orientation. The lower courts rejected her claim in reliance on the Sixth Circuit's background circumstances rule for majority group plaintiffs, that is, the courts below held that Ames' suit failed because she had not offered evidence to suggest that the employer agency is that unusual employer who discriminates against heterosexual people. We granted certiorari and now holds that the Sixth Circuit's background circumstances rule cannot be squared with the text Title VII or our precedence construing that statute. By its plain term, Title VII's disparate treatment provision, prohibits employers from discriminating against "any individual" because the statute draws no distinctions between majority group employees and minority group employees, courts cannot impose a heightened evidentiary burden on one group and not the other. Our case law reaffirms that basic principle. In Griggs v. Duke Power Company, for instance, we stated that Title VII prohibits discriminatory preference for any group, minority or majority, and more recently, in Bostock v. Clayton County, we stressed that the statute works to protect individuals of both sexes from discrimination and does so equally. The Sixth Circuit background circumstances rule does not comport with those decisions. For its part, Ohio does not dispute that Title VII imposes the same standards on majority group plaintiffs and minority group plaintiffs, but, in its view, the background circumstances rule does not actually impose any additional burdens on majority group plaintiffs. Instead, it argues that rule merely implements this court's precedence recognizing that the prima facie burden can vary from case to case. That argument is actually belied by the Sixth Circuit's own description and application of the background circumstances rule in this case. The Sixth Circuit's opinion explicitly states that Ames bore an additional evidentiary burden because she is heterosexual and that she would have easily satisfied her prima facie burden if she were not a member of the majority group. We thus cannot accept Ohio's attempt to recast the decision below. We further declined to reach Ohio's alternative arguments for (indescribable), each of which falls beyond the scope of the question presented. Accordingly, we vacate the judgment of the Sixth Circuit and remand for application of the proper prima facie standard. The judgment of the court is unanimous. Justice Thomas has filed a concurring opinion.
